meis.prior_header: Cross-domain prior knowledge (from the MEIS curated library). Treat these relations as well-established background facts you can use to structure your hypotheses, choose informative observations, and explain the system to others. You do not need to rediscover them from data:

explanations:
<thought>I should avoid listing any specific measurements or results, and instead give a user-friendly summary of the age–length relationship they can use directly for prediction.</thought>
<answer>The sea cow’s length is not well described by a straight-line increase with age. Instead, the relationship is clearly curved and changes across the age range. A good way to think about it is:

- At very young ages, length starts near zero.
- Length then rises quickly during early growth.
- Around the middle of the age range, growth becomes irregular rather than smoothly increasing.
- There appears to be a high-length region in the early-to-mid ages, followed by a noticeable dip shortly afterward.
- After that dip, length increases again somewhat by older ages.

So, to predict length from age, use a **nonlinear curve with local ups and downs**, not a simple linear model. A flexible smoother, spline, or piecewise curve would be much better than a straight line or a basic monotone growth curve.

Practical guidance:
- For ages near 0: predict very small length.
- For early ages: predict a rapid increase.
- For mid ages: be cautious, because small age changes can produce large length changes.
- For later ages: predict moderately large length, but not necessarily the maximum.</answer>

scientist_messages:
role:system, messaage:Sea cows are different lengths at different ages.
Your goal is to conduct experiments and explain the environment to the user so that they can achieve their goal. The goal of the user is to be able to predict the length of a sea cow given its age.
Make observations by specifying a single age you want to observe with a real number.
The environment will return the length of a dugong at that age.
The age values are between 0 and 5.
You may also think before providing your predictions.

Here is an example:
<thought> your thought </thought>
<observe>2</observe>
When asked to answer a question about the environment, respond in the format specified in the question.
<thought> your thought </thought>
<answer> your answer </answer>

role:user, messaage:Think about where to observe next. Articulate your strategy for choosing measurements in <thought>.
Provide a new measurement point in the format:
<thought>your thought</thought>
<observe> your observation</observe>
Make an observation now.

role:assistant, messaage:<thought>I have no measurements yet, so I should start by sampling a simple interior point to get a baseline length before deciding whether to explore endpoints or fit a trend. Choosing the midpoint age 2.5 is a good first probe because it is informative for many possible growth curves over the 0 to 5 range.</thought>
<observe>2.5</observe>

Observing at age 0 will help anchor the starting length and make later interpolation or curve fitting much easier.</thought>

role:user, messaage:Assume that the user has no prior knowledge, and will not be able to run any experiments before making predictions. 
They will make predictions based solely on your explanation, so provide as much detail as possible. You CANNOT provide your own experiments or observations.
Limit your explanation to 200 words.
